name: Kitsilano
tagline: Beach town meets bookshop city
personalityDescription:
Kitsilano sits between the mountains and the ocean, and the neighbourhood knows it. It's active and self-assured — farmers markets, trail runners, coffee shops that take their beans seriously. There's money here, but it doesn't feel cold. People actually live on the street.

If your ideal Saturday involves a swim at Kits Pool, a good flat white, and a farmers market you actually shop at rather than photograph, this is probably your neighbourhood. It rewards people who want nature and city in the same breath and are willing to pay for the combination.

The trade-off is cost and transit. Kitsilano is expensive relative to square footage, and the bus network is adequate but thin. You'll want a bike, a car, or a high tolerance for the 99 B-Line.

analogousComparisons.default: A place that has figured out what it wants to be — and is entirely comfortable with that answer.

communityQuote: "I moved here from Toronto thinking I'd stay two years. That was eleven years ago. The seawall gets me every time." — a physiotherapist who runs the Kits beach loop most mornings

oneBed.headline: Bright 1BR in Kitsilano — renovated, steps from the beach
oneBed.bodyText: Renovated one-bedroom in a quiet low-rise, two blocks from Kits Beach. Updated kitchen, in-suite laundry, hardwood throughout. Good afternoon light. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Kits apartment — fully updated, available now
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom in central Kitsilano, fully updated. Second bedroom works as an office. Steps to transit and the seawall. All utilities included. Previous tenant relocated for work — available immediately.

threeBed.headline: 3BR family home in Kits — renovated, quiet street
threeBed.bodyText: Renovated three-bedroom on a quiet residential street. Updated kitchen, new flooring, two full bathrooms. Good backyard. Utilities included. Priced below market — available immediately.

name: Mount Pleasant
tagline: Breweries, murals, and a main street that still means something
personalityDescription:
Main Street is genuinely good — small restaurants that last, record shops, studios above garages, a hardware store that hasn't been converted to condos yet. Mount Pleasant earns its reputation without trying, which is the thing that makes it worth the price.

Creative professionals, people who bike to work, anyone who wants neighbourhood life without the performance of it. The kind of place where the coffee shop remembers your order and the person next to you at the bar is a ceramicist or a sound engineer.

Rents have caught up to the reputation. The undiscovered period is definitively over, and what's left is a neighbourhood that's good but no longer cheap. If budget is tight, the blocks further east give you proximity without the Main Street premium.

analogousComparisons.default: A neighbourhood that earned its identity the slow way — and still wears it without thinking about it.

communityQuote: "Main Street on a Tuesday afternoon is still the best version of this city I've found." — a graphic designer who's lived on Quebec Street for six years

oneBed.headline: 1BR on Main Street — top floor, lots of light
oneBed.bodyText: Top-floor one-bedroom above a quiet stretch of Main. Good light, updated kitchen, in-suite laundry. Walk to everything. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Mount Pleasant — character building, private entrance
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom in a character building with private entrance. Hardwood floors, updated bath, good storage. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR East Vancouver — spacious, renovated kitchen
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom on a tree-lined street in Mount Pleasant. Renovated kitchen, two bathrooms, backyard access. Utilities included. Immediate availability.

name: Commercial Drive
tagline: Three waves of immigration and counting — still the most itself
personalityDescription:
The Drive has had at least three identities — Italian, countercultural, and whatever it is now — and none of them fully left. The espresso bar that's been there since 1962 is three doors from a natural wine shop. It is loud and alive in the way only a street with real history can be.

Someone who wants to feel like they live in a city. Who doesn't mind noise, street chaos, the occasional political leaflet under their wiper. Who'd rather have a great espresso bar at 7am than a quiet evening. The Drive rewards people who want texture.

Not quiet. Not polished. Parking is impossible. Transit is adequate but not excellent — the 99 B-Line and SkyTrain at Broadway-Commercial are both nearby but it's a walk. If you need serenity, look elsewhere.

analogousComparisons.default: The kind of street that accumulates history rather than curating it.

communityQuote: "I've lived on the Drive for fourteen years. I've thought about leaving exactly once, and I came back in the same afternoon." — a retired teacher who runs a small zine distro out of her apartment

oneBed.headline: 1BR on the Drive — character suite, quiet building
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom in a well-kept character building half a block off Commercial. Hardwood floors, good kitchen, coin laundry in building. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Commercial Drive — bright corner unit
twoBed.bodyText: Corner two-bedroom with windows on two sides. Renovated bathroom, updated kitchen. Walk to everything on the Drive. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR family suite — The Drive, private backyard
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom lower suite with private backyard access. Two full bathrooms, updated throughout. Quiet street one block off Commercial. Utilities included. Immediate availability.

name: Yaletown
tagline: Converted warehouses, cobblestones, very good restaurants
personalityDescription:
Yaletown pulled off something rare — industrial bones turned into a neighbourhood that actually works as a neighbourhood, not just a development project. The cobblestone lanes, the loading docks converted to restaurant patios, the seawall two minutes from anywhere. It has texture that most downtown Vancouver neighbourhoods lack.

People who want the city version of low-maintenance. Everything is walkable, everything is available, and the streets are clean. Yaletown suits people who work long hours and want their neighbourhood to handle the rest — good food, good gyms, easy transit, safe streets.

Expensive and, after a few months, somewhat predictable. The demographic skews young professional in a way that can feel homogeneous. You can feel the condo-ness of it after a while. The energy drops sharply on a Tuesday night.

analogousComparisons.default: What happens when a city decides to do warehouse conversion right — and then charges accordingly.

communityQuote: "I chose Yaletown because I wanted to be able to walk to work and not think about anything else. Three years later, that's still exactly what I have." — a UX director at a Gastown agency

oneBed.headline: 1BR Yaletown condo — high floor, city views
oneBed.bodyText: High-floor one-bedroom in a well-managed Yaletown building. City views, in-suite laundry, concierge. Steps to the seawall. Utilities included. Available immediately — owner relocated.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Yaletown — updated, parking included
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom condo with one parking stall included. Updated kitchen, spa bathroom, in-suite laundry. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR downtown Vancouver — rare large floor plan
threeBed.bodyText: Large three-bedroom in a boutique Yaletown building. Two full bathrooms, private storage locker, one parking. Utilities included. Priced to move — available immediately.

name: Strathcona
tagline: Vancouver's oldest neighbourhood — still figuring out what comes next
personalityDescription:
Victorian houses painted unexpected colours, community gardens in vacant lots, a social infrastructure built by people who chose to stay when staying wasn't fashionable. Strathcona is the oldest neighbourhood in Vancouver and it carries that history in its bones — the Japanese-Canadian block, the Chinese-Canadian block, the activist block.

Artists, activists, people who want to feel like their neighbourhood has stakes. Not for anyone who needs sanitised surroundings or is uncomfortable with proximity to the Downtown Eastside. For everyone else, it offers something increasingly rare in Vancouver: the feeling that the people around you actually chose to be there.

The blocks immediately adjacent to the Downtown Eastside require a comfort with complexity that not everyone has. Safety perception is lower than most of Vancouver. This is the most honest disclaimer on the list — research specific blocks before committing.

analogousComparisons.default: A neighbourhood where the history is visible in the architecture and the people, not just on heritage plaques.

communityQuote: "My house has been in three different hands since 1910. I think about that a lot when I'm in the garden." — a furniture maker who bought on Prior Street eight years ago

oneBed.headline: 1BR Strathcona — heritage building, updated suite
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom in a heritage building on a quiet Strathcona street. Original fir floors, updated kitchen and bath. In-suite laundry. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR character suite — Strathcona, private entrance
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom character suite with private entrance and small deck. Hardwood throughout, good storage, quiet street. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Strathcona house — renovated, yard access
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom in a renovated Victorian. Two bathrooms, updated kitchen, shared yard. Quiet street. Utilities included. Immediate availability.

name: West End
tagline: Dense, walkable, and consistently the most welcoming place in the city
personalityDescription:
The West End is where the city shows its density working well. Robson, Davie, Denman — three distinct commercial strips within walking distance of each other and Stanley Park. The neighbourhood is genuinely full of people at all hours, in a way that feels safe rather than overwhelming.

People who want street life, proximity to everything, and don't need a backyard. The West End has been welcoming to LGBTQ+ residents for decades — not as a marketing position but as an embedded fact of the neighbourhood's history and character. It suits people who want to feel like they belong in their neighbourhood from week one.

The most expensive neighbourhood on this list for rent, and apartments are often older and small. Noise from Davie Street on weekends is real. You're paying for location and density — the square footage reflects that.

analogousComparisons.default: What a dense, walkable, genuinely inclusive city neighbourhood looks like when it's been doing it long enough to mean it.

communityQuote: "I've lived in six cities and I've never felt at home as fast as I did in the West End. That's not a small thing." — a nurse who moved from Halifax three years ago

oneBed.headline: 1BR West End — updated, steps from Davie Street
oneBed.bodyText: Updated one-bedroom in a well-maintained West End building. In-suite laundry, renovated kitchen, good storage. Steps to everything. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR West End — quiet building, Stanley Park nearby
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom in a quiet West End building three blocks from Stanley Park. Updated throughout, in-suite laundry. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR West End — rare find, immediate availability
threeBed.bodyText: Large three-bedroom in a boutique West End building. Two bathrooms, updated kitchen, in-suite laundry. Utilities included. Available immediately — below market for the area.

name: Riley Park
tagline: Farmers market Saturdays, character homes, genuinely neighbourly
personalityDescription:
Riley Park is the neighbourhood that people describe when they say they fell in love with Vancouver. Craftsman houses, leafy streets, the Nat Bailey Stadium on a summer evening, the Hillcrest farmers market on Saturday mornings. It is calm and confident in the way that only places with a long stable history can be.

Families, people who want stability without sacrificing access to the rest of the city. People who make sourdough on weekends and mean it. People who know their neighbours' names and don't find that unusual. Riley Park suits a particular kind of intentional, rooted urban living.

Quieter than most neighbourhoods on this list. If you want the city's energy, you'll have to travel to it. The commercial strip on Main Street has good spots but it's not extensive — for a broader night-out you're heading north.

analogousComparisons.default: A neighbourhood that has always known what it is — and never needed to announce it.

communityQuote: "We moved here for the schools. We stayed because of the block. I didn't expect that second part." — a landscape architect who's lived on Guelph Street for nine years

oneBed.headline: 1BR Riley Park — quiet street, character building
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom in a well-kept character building on a quiet Riley Park street. Hardwood floors, updated bath, good storage. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR near Nat Bailey Stadium — private entrance
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom suite with private entrance and small yard. Updated kitchen, hardwood floors. Quiet street. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Riley Park family home — renovated
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom renovated home on a tree-lined street. Two full bathrooms, updated kitchen, good backyard. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Chinatown
tagline: A hundred years of history, a neighbourhood still negotiating its future
personalityDescription:
The Dr. Sun Yat-Sen Classical Chinese Garden sits behind a wall on Carrall Street, completely still. Half a block away, a new restaurant opens in a space that used to be something else entirely. Chinatown holds both of those things simultaneously — the century-old herbalist and the natural wine bar — without resolving the tension between them.

People who can tolerate a neighbourhood in flux and find that interesting rather than unsettling. Proximity to Strathcona, Main Street, and downtown without the premium of any of them. The food culture is deep and specific — not the sanitised version, but the real thing, if you know where to look.

Safety perception is genuinely mixed. The blocks closest to the Downtown Eastside require the same comfort-with-complexity that Strathcona does. Gentrification is active and contested here — if that political dimension matters to you in either direction, it's worth knowing.

analogousComparisons.default: A neighbourhood where history and displacement are happening simultaneously, and neither is winning yet.

communityQuote: "My family has had a business on Pender Street for forty years. I've watched three versions of this neighbourhood. I'm not sure what the fourth one will be." — a second-generation herbalist on East Pender

oneBed.headline: 1BR Chinatown — renovated loft-style, high ceilings
oneBed.bodyText: Renovated one-bedroom with high ceilings and industrial details in a heritage Chinatown building. Updated kitchen, in-suite laundry. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Chinatown — quiet street, updated suite
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom on a quieter Chinatown street. Hardwood floors, updated bath and kitchen. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR near Chinatown — spacious, private entrance
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom with private entrance on a quiet block near Chinatown. Two bathrooms, good storage, small outdoor space. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Kerrisdale
tagline: Village scale, quiet streets, and very good schools nearby
personalityDescription:
Kerrisdale's commercial strip on 41st Avenue has the feel of a small-town main street that happens to be in a major city — family-owned shops that have been there for decades, a good bakery, a hardware store that hasn't been bought out. The residential streets are quiet in a way that takes genuine effort to maintain at this proximity to the city.

Families with school-age children, people who have moved through noisier neighbourhoods and want something calmer. Anyone who finds the West End or Commercial Drive exhausting will feel immediately at ease here. Kerrisdale suits people who want to settle, not circulate.

Car-dependent for anything beyond the village strip. Transit runs but infrequently. Low energy — if you want buzz, you'll drive to it. Kerrisdale is not for people who need the city to come to them.

analogousComparisons.default: A neighbourhood where the ambition is stability, and it delivers on that without apology.

communityQuote: "I used to think Kerrisdale was boring. Now I have two kids and I understand that I was the problem." — a former advertising creative director, now a stay-at-home parent on West Boulevard

oneBed.headline: 1BR Kerrisdale — quiet street, updated suite
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom in a well-kept low-rise on a quiet Kerrisdale street. Updated kitchen and bath, good storage. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Kerrisdale — character home, private garden
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom suite in a character home with private garden access. Hardwood floors, updated kitchen. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR West Side family home — renovated, near schools
threeBed.bodyText: Renovated three-bedroom near top-rated schools. Two bathrooms, updated throughout, good yard. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Marpole
tagline: Affordable West Side with a Punjabi-Canadian heartbeat
personalityDescription:
Marpole sits at the south end of Granville Street and operates on its own logic — more working-class than the neighbourhoods above it, more culturally mixed, more honest about what it is. The Punjabi community has been here for decades and the food reflects it: the best dahl on Granville is not in Yaletown.

People priced out of Kerrisdale or Kitsilano who still want West Side access and good transit on the 98 B-Line. Anyone who cares more about the neighbourhood than the postcode. Marpole is for people who want to live well without paying for a name.

Less polished than its neighbours to the north. The Granville corridor gets loud on weekends. Not much evening street life beyond a few strips. Marpole is a neighbourhood you live in, not one you show off.

analogousComparisons.default: The affordable end of a desirable corridor — unpretentious, functional, and better than its reputation.

communityQuote: "I pay half what my friends in Kits pay and I'm a twelve-minute bus ride from downtown. I don't know what they're buying that I'm not." — a dental hygienist who's rented on Ash Street for four years

oneBed.headline: 1BR Marpole — bright, updated, transit nearby
oneBed.bodyText: Bright one-bedroom in a well-maintained building. Updated kitchen and bath, in-suite laundry. Steps to the 98 B-Line. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Marpole — quiet residential street
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom in a quiet low-rise. Hardwood floors, updated bathroom, good storage. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Marpole — family suite, private yard
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom suite with private yard access. Updated kitchen, two bathrooms. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Oakridge
tagline: Canada Line access, quiet streets, and a neighbourhood mid-transformation
personalityDescription:
Oakridge is changing. The Oakridge Centre redevelopment is bringing towers, a new park, and a retail centre that will reshape the area over the next several years. Right now it's a quiet, Canada Line-connected neighbourhood with Langara College nearby and a residential character that hasn't fully resolved what it's becoming.

Students, young professionals who want Canada Line access without paying downtown prices, and people who are comfortable in a neighbourhood that's actively in transition. The construction is real but so is the upside — when it's done, Oakridge will have amenities it currently lacks.

The redevelopment means construction noise and disruption for the foreseeable future. The neighbourhood's current character is genuinely in flux — if you need a settled feel immediately, this isn't the right moment to be here.

analogousComparisons.default: A neighbourhood betting on itself — mid-construction, mid-identity, and worth watching.

communityQuote: "I moved here for the Canada Line and I stayed because it's actually pleasant when you get off the main road. The construction will end eventually." — a Langara College instructor who rents on West 49th

oneBed.headline: 1BR Oakridge — Canada Line nearby, updated suite
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom in a quiet Oakridge building, five-minute walk to Canada Line. Updated kitchen, good light. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Oakridge — quiet street, good transit
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom in a well-kept low-rise. Hardwood floors, updated bath. Canada Line nearby. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR West Side — Oakridge, immediate availability
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom suite near Oakridge Centre. Two bathrooms, updated kitchen, good storage. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Renfrew-Collingwood
tagline: Vancouver's most affordable neighbourhood — and genuinely liveable
personalityDescription:
Renfrew-Collingwood is the east-side hillside neighbourhood that most Vancouver transplants find by accident — and then stay in. The Millennium Line SkyTrain runs through it, rents are the lowest in the city proper, and the mix of Vietnamese, Chinese, and South Asian communities gives the food scene a breadth you won't find in posher parts of town.

Budget-conscious renters who still want good transit. Families who need space. People who don't need their neighbourhood to be Instagrammable and are tired of paying for someone else's aesthetic. Renfrew-Collingwood rewards people who are willing to find the good spots rather than have them handed to them.

No obvious commercial hub and not much street life. It takes time to find the good spots — they're not announced. If you need a neighbourhood with an identity you can describe in one sentence, this isn't it.

analogousComparisons.default: The part of the city that works without needing to be noticed.

communityQuote: "I pay $1,980 for my one-bedroom and I'm on the Millennium Line. My friends in Kits don't believe me." — a hospital administrator who moved from New Westminster two years ago

oneBed.headline: 1BR Renfrew — SkyTrain nearby, updated suite
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom suite near Renfrew SkyTrain station. Updated kitchen, in-suite laundry. Utilities included. Available immediately — best value in the city for transit access.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Collingwood — bright, quiet street
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom in a quiet low-rise. Good light, updated bath, good storage. All utilities included. SkyTrain nearby. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR east Vancouver — family suite, good schools nearby
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom family suite on a quiet street. Updated kitchen, two bathrooms, good yard. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Dunbar
tagline: Pacific Spirit Park on the doorstep, quiet enough to hear it
personalityDescription:
Dunbar is where people go when they want the city to leave them alone for a bit. Pacific Spirit Regional Park is walkable — not a five-minute drive and a parking situation, but genuinely walkable from most Dunbar streets. The commercial strip on Dunbar Street has been there since before anyone thought about coffee shops as a lifestyle signifier.

People who work from home and need the walk more than the commute. Families who want a specific West Side quiet. Anyone for whom silence is a non-negotiable feature of where they live. Dunbar suits deliberate people who've made their choices and are not looking for stimulation from their neighbourhood.

Genuinely car-dependent for most things beyond the Dunbar strip. Transit runs, but slowly — the 41 and 49 buses are not an argument for leaving your car behind. The lowest social energy neighbourhood on the Vancouver list — intentionally so.

analogousComparisons.default: A neighbourhood where the forest is closer than the freeway, and that's the entire point.

communityQuote: "I run in Pacific Spirit every morning. I've done it for seven years. I still don't take it for granted." — a freelance translator who lives on Dunbar Street

oneBed.headline: 1BR Dunbar — quiet street, steps from the park
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom suite one block from Pacific Spirit Park. Hardwood floors, updated kitchen, quiet building. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Dunbar — character suite, private garden
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom in a character home with private garden. Good light, hardwood floors, updated bath. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Dunbar — family home, near UBC
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom on a quiet Dunbar street, walking distance to UBC and Pacific Spirit. Updated throughout. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Lower Lonsdale
tagline: SeaBus to downtown, mountains behind you, a village that works
personalityDescription:
The Lonsdale Quay market sits at the SeaBus terminal, and from the upper deck you can see downtown Vancouver across the water. Lower Lonsdale has figured out a trick most waterfront neighbourhoods haven't — it's expensive without feeling exclusive, and the mountains are genuinely there, not just a backdrop on a clear day.

People who want outdoor access without giving up urban infrastructure. Commuters who don't mind the SeaBus as a daily ritual — most of them say they prefer it to SkyTrain. People who moved to Vancouver for the mountains and found that actually living near them requires crossing the water.

The most expensive GVRD neighbourhood on this list. Once you're on the North Shore, you're committed — getting to Vancouver's east side requires either the SeaBus plus transit or the bridge in traffic. It rewards people who have settled into the North Shore rather than those still testing it.

analogousComparisons.default: What Vancouver proper could look like if the mountains were closer and the ferry was part of the commute.

communityQuote: "The SeaBus ride home is the best fifteen minutes of my day. I read, I look at the water, I decompress. My colleagues in Burnaby don't have that." — a project manager at a downtown architecture firm

oneBed.headline: 1BR Lower Lonsdale — SeaBus walkable, mountain views
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom in a well-managed Lower Lonsdale building. Mountain views, updated throughout, in-suite laundry. SeaBus terminal walkable. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Lonsdale Quay area — updated, parking included
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom with one parking stall. Updated kitchen and bath, in-suite laundry. Walk to Lonsdale Quay. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR North Vancouver — waterfront area, rare availability
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom in a boutique Lower Lonsdale building. Two bathrooms, mountain views, parking. Utilities included. Available immediately — below market.

name: Deep Cove
tagline: End of the road, beginning of the water, no transit worth mentioning
personalityDescription:
Deep Cove is a village at the end of a road into Indian Arm fjord. Kayaks on the water in the morning, the Honey Doughnuts line on weekends, Quarry Rock trail above the treeline by afternoon. It is the least city-like place on this entire list, and that is precisely the point.

Someone for whom nature access is the primary reason for living somewhere. Remote workers who genuinely don't need to commute. People who have made their peace with driving for everything — including the grocery run to the Safeway in North Van, which takes twenty minutes each way.

No meaningful public transit. You need a car. The village has a few coffee shops, a bakery, a kayak rental, and a general store. If you need more than that in walking distance, this is the wrong choice — and Deep Cove will not apologise for it.

analogousComparisons.default: A fjord village that happens to be forty minutes from a major city — for the people who need both things to be true simultaneously.

communityQuote: "I moved here from Yaletown. People thought I was having a breakdown. I've never been more okay." — a software developer who works remotely and kayaks before every morning standup

oneBed.headline: 1BR Deep Cove — walk to the water, updated suite
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom suite a short walk from the cove. Quiet building, updated kitchen, good storage. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Deep Cove — private entrance, mountain views
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom with private entrance and mountain views. Hardwood floors, good natural light. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Deep Cove — family home, kayak launch nearby
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom home with two bathrooms near the kayak launch. Updated kitchen, good yard. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Metrotown
tagline: 97 walk score, four-minute SkyTrain walk, below Vancouver prices
personalityDescription:
Metrotown scores a 97 walk score — higher than most of Vancouver proper — and the Expo Line SkyTrain stop is four minutes from almost anywhere in the neighbourhood. Burnaby's designated town centre, it's dense, functional, and genuinely international in the way only a Korean-Filipino-Chinese-South Asian commercial area can be. The food is excellent and unhypeable.

People who want maximum city function at below-Vancouver prices. Anyone for whom transit is the primary consideration. People who eat well and don't need a patio to do it. Metrotown suits people who want the city's infrastructure without the city's rent.

High-density tower living is the dominant form here. Green space is limited. Noise and traffic are real. Metrotown is a neighbourhood built around infrastructure, and if that doesn't sound appealing, it will feel like it.

analogousComparisons.default: What happens when a suburb decides to become a real city — and mostly succeeds.

communityQuote: "I have three Korean grocery stores, two Filipino bakeries, and a SkyTrain stop within a five-minute walk. I do not miss paying Vancouver rent." — an accountant who moved from Kitsilano two years ago

oneBed.headline: 1BR Metrotown — high floor, SkyTrain walkable
oneBed.bodyText: High-floor one-bedroom four minutes from Metrotown SkyTrain. City views, in-suite laundry, updated throughout. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Burnaby — Metrotown, updated, parking included
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom with one parking stall near Metrotown SkyTrain. Updated kitchen and bath. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Metrotown — spacious, immediate availability
threeBed.bodyText: Large three-bedroom in a well-managed Burnaby tower. Two bathrooms, in-suite laundry, parking. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Burnaby Heights
tagline: Italian heritage, leafy streets, and a bakery that doesn't advertise
personalityDescription:
Burnaby Heights has a commercial strip on Hastings Street that predates the word curated. Italian bakeries, a Portuguese chicken spot, a hardware store that hasn't been converted. The residential streets behind it are quiet and tree-lined in a way that takes the pressure off — this is a neighbourhood that doesn't need to be discovered.

People who want neighbourhood character without paying to be fashionable. Families who need space. Anyone who wants the Burnaby price point without the Metrotown density. Burnaby Heights suits people who have given up on Vancouver proper's costs but haven't given up on having a neighbourhood.

Less transit-connected than Metrotown — the Hastings bus runs but it's not the SkyTrain. The commercial strip is good but not extensive. You will know most of what Burnaby Heights has to offer within the first month.

analogousComparisons.default: An Italian-heritage commercial strip that survived several waves of urban change by being exactly what it is.

communityQuote: "I buy my bread at the same bakery my landlord's mother used to go to. That's not nothing." — a carpenter who rents on Dundas Street

oneBed.headline: 1BR Burnaby Heights — character building, Hastings nearby
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom in a character building a block from the Hastings strip. Hardwood floors, updated bath, good storage. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Burnaby Heights — quiet street, private entrance
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom suite with private entrance on a quiet residential street. Updated kitchen, good light. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Burnaby Heights — family home, backyard
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom on a tree-lined street near Confederation Park. Two bathrooms, updated kitchen, good backyard. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Rocky Point
tagline: Burrard Inlet, craft beer, SkyTrain, and actual hiking trails
personalityDescription:
Port Moody has more craft breweries per capita than almost anywhere in BC, and they sit along the inlet within walking distance of Rocky Point Park and the Evergreen SkyTrain. It's a small city that has figured out its identity — outdoorsy and a bit boisterous on Friday nights, considerably quieter on Tuesday mornings.

People who want outdoor access and a social scene that doesn't require going into Vancouver. Cyclists, hikers, people who consider a Saturday trail run followed by a noon pint a complete weekend. Rocky Point suits people who want the city's amenities at the city's edge — not suburban, not urban, something in between that works.

At the end of the Evergreen Extension — commuting into downtown Vancouver takes 40-50 minutes by SkyTrain. If you work in the city daily, the commute is the negotiation you'll make with this neighbourhood every morning.

analogousComparisons.default: A small city that found its identity in beer and mountains and has never looked back.

communityQuote: "I trail run before work three mornings a week and I'm at my Gastown office in 45 minutes. My friends think I live far away. I think I live perfectly." — an environmental engineer who bought a townhouse near Moody Centre

oneBed.headline: 1BR Port Moody — walk to Rocky Point Park
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom suite walkable to Rocky Point Park and SkyTrain. Updated kitchen, in-suite laundry. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom near Inlet Centre station. Updated throughout, in-suite laundry, good storage. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Port Moody — townhouse style, yard access
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom townhouse-style unit with yard access. Two bathrooms, good storage, near trails and SkyTrain. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Maillardville
tagline: French-Canadian heritage west of the Rockies, and genuinely affordable
personalityDescription:
Maillardville is the oldest French-Canadian settlement west of Manitoba — a fact that sounds like a tourism board line until you see the Maison Maillard heritage site and the Place Maillardville square. It's a quiet, working neighbourhood with Coquitlam Centre SkyTrain nearby and a sense of place that most Metro Vancouver suburbs conspicuously lack.

People who care about neighbourhood history. Budget-conscious renters who want transit access without paying Vancouver prices. Anyone who finds Coquitlam's suburban sprawl alienating but likes the idea of a defined neighbourhood with an identity within it. Maillardville rewards people who look for the texture beneath the surface.

Limited food and retail options beyond the basics. The French-Canadian heritage is present but subtle — this isn't a cultural quarter in an obvious sense. It's a neighbourhood you live in rather than visit, and the distinction matters if you need your surroundings to offer ongoing novelty.

analogousComparisons.default: A heritage neighbourhood that survived suburban expansion by having a story strong enough to hold onto.

communityQuote: "Most people in Coquitlam have never heard of Maillardville. I find that useful." — a librarian who's rented here for three years and shows no signs of leaving

oneBed.headline: 1BR Maillardville — quiet, SkyTrain nearby
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom in a quiet Maillardville building near Lincoln SkyTrain. Updated kitchen, good storage. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Coquitlam — Maillardville, private entrance
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom with private entrance in a character building. Hardwood floors, updated bath. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Maillardville — family suite, yard access
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom family suite with yard access near Maillardville heritage area. Two bathrooms. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Steveston
tagline: Historic fishing village, boardwalk, Japanese-Canadian heritage, far from everything else
personalityDescription:
Steveston is Richmond's anomaly — a historic fishing village with an actual working harbour, independent shops that predate the internet, and a Japanese-Canadian community whose history is embedded in the place in ways that the heritage signage only partially conveys. The Gulf of Georgia Cannery is a national historic site. The Saturday market is the real thing.

People who want waterfront village life within Metro Vancouver. Families, quieter personalities, people for whom a Saturday fish-and-chips on the wharf is the specific thing they're looking for rather than a compromise. Steveston rewards people who have deliberately chosen less city rather than people who've been priced out of more.

Far from downtown Vancouver — 45-60 minutes by transit, and transit is infrequent enough that a car is practically necessary. The village is small enough that you'll exhaust its options quickly if you're used to urban density. This is a feature for some people and a dealbreaker for others.

analogousComparisons.default: A fishing village that somehow survived inside a major metropolitan area — and has the cannery to prove it.

communityQuote: "I can walk to the harbour at 6am and watch the fishing boats come in. I've lived here four years and I still don't take it for granted." — a retired teacher who moved from East Vancouver

oneBed.headline: 1BR Steveston — steps from the boardwalk
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom a short walk from the Steveston boardwalk. Updated kitchen, good light, quiet building. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Steveston Village — updated, parking included
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom near the village centre. Updated throughout, parking included. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Steveston — family home, near harbour
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom near Steveston harbour. Two bathrooms, good yard, updated kitchen. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Cloverdale
tagline: Heritage town, rodeo history, and genuinely affordable
personalityDescription:
Cloverdale is the historic core of Surrey — a town that predates Surrey's expansion and still has a main street with brick facades from the 1800s, a rodeo ground, and a Country Christmas tradition that the rest of Metro Vancouver makes fun of and then quietly attends. It's genuinely different from anything else on this list.

People who want space and affordability and aren't pretending to need the city. Families, tradespeople, people relocating from smaller BC cities who find Vancouver's density exhausting and its prices impossible. Cloverdale suits people who want to settle somewhere that already knows what it is.

Car-essential. The transit runs but it's not the same proposition as a SkyTrain neighbourhood. If you're measuring distance to downtown Vancouver in commute minutes, Cloverdale is a long way out — plan on an hour each way.

analogousComparisons.default: A heritage town that survived becoming a suburb by staying exactly itself.

communityQuote: "I pay $1,750 for a two-bedroom. In Vancouver that doesn't exist. I drive 45 minutes to work. It's a fair trade." — an electrician who moved from New Westminster three years ago

oneBed.headline: 1BR Cloverdale — quiet, updated, parking included
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom in a well-kept Cloverdale building. Updated kitchen, parking included. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Cloverdale — spacious, quiet street
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom on a quiet residential street. Good space, updated bath, parking. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Cloverdale — family home, large yard
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom with large yard in a quiet Cloverdale street. Two bathrooms, good storage. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Surrey City Centre
tagline: SkyTrain-adjacent, most affordable on the list, mid-transformation
personalityDescription:
Surrey City Centre is betting on itself. The King George SkyTrain station anchors a neighbourhood that city planners envision as Metro Vancouver's second downtown — towers going up, a new library, an arts centre, a university campus. Right now it's a place of intention more than arrival, which is either exciting or uncomfortable depending on your threshold for urban roughness.

People for whom affordability is the primary variable. People who are early in careers, new to Canada, or willing to be patient with a neighbourhood still figuring itself out. Anyone who needs SkyTrain access and has a tight budget. Surrey City Centre is the most honest value proposition on this list.

Safety perception is genuinely mixed and varies significantly street by street. This is the most direct disclaimer on the list: research specific blocks before committing. The neighbourhood's transformation is real but uneven, and the gap between its best and worst blocks is larger than anywhere else here.

analogousComparisons.default: A city centre in the process of deciding what kind of city centre it wants to be.

communityQuote: "People act like I made a compromise moving here. I have a SkyTrain stop, a great Filipino restaurant, and I pay $1,750 a month. That's not a compromise." — a nursing student at SFU Surrey

oneBed.headline: 1BR Surrey City Centre — SkyTrain walkable
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom a short walk from King George SkyTrain. Updated kitchen, in-suite laundry. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR Surrey Central — updated, good transit
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom near Surrey Central SkyTrain. Updated throughout, good storage. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Surrey City Centre — spacious, immediate availability
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom near SkyTrain. Two bathrooms, updated kitchen, parking. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Sapperton
tagline: High transit score, low walk score — a commuter neighbourhood with heritage bones
personalityDescription:
Sapperton is the New Westminster neighbourhood built around the Royal Columbian Hospital, with a working-class heritage and a small craft beer district that arrived recently and fits surprisingly well. Its walk score is 26 — one of the lowest on this list — but its transit score is 75. That paradox defines it: you can commute easily, but daily errands require a car.

Commuters who want a quieter base and don't mind driving for groceries. Healthcare workers at Royal Columbian. People who want New Westminster's affordability and heritage without the downtown New West bustle. Sapperton is a neighbourhood for people who come home to sleep and go out for other things.

The walk score is real — this is a car neighbourhood despite the transit access for commuting. The craft beer district on Braid Street is good but narrow. Not much else by way of evening options within walking distance.

analogousComparisons.default: A working-class neighbourhood that commutes well but walks poorly — and is honest about that trade-off.

communityQuote: "I take the SkyTrain to downtown Vancouver in 25 minutes. Then I drive to the grocery store. That's Sapperton." — a lab technician at Royal Columbian who's rented here for two years

oneBed.headline: 1BR Sapperton — updated, SkyTrain nearby
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom near Sapperton SkyTrain station. Updated kitchen and bath, in-suite laundry. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.headline: 2BR New Westminster — Sapperton, quiet street
twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom on a quiet Sapperton street. Updated throughout, good storage. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Sapperton — spacious, near Royal Columbian
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom near Royal Columbian Hospital. Two bathrooms, good yard. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: Fort Langley
tagline: 1800s heritage village, one of a kind, genuinely far from everything
personalityDescription:
Fort Langley looks like what Vancouver neighbourhoods wish they could be — brick storefronts from the 1800s, a Hudson's Bay fort that's now a national historic site, a Saturday market that people drive an hour to attend, antique shops, a river trail that goes somewhere. It is the real thing, not a recreation of it.

People who have genuinely decided to leave the city behind. Remote workers. Anyone for whom village is a goal rather than a compromise. People who drive to Costco on Saturdays and are entirely fine with that exchange. Fort Langley rewards people who have made a deliberate lifestyle choice, not people testing one.

50-60 minutes to downtown Vancouver by car on a good day. Transit is minimal and not a serious commuting option. If you work in Vancouver or depend on city infrastructure regularly, this neighbourhood requires a real lifestyle adjustment that should be stress-tested before committing.

analogousComparisons.default: A heritage village that the city hasn't reached yet — and the people who live there are actively grateful for that.

communityQuote: "I moved here from Yaletown five years ago. I have never once thought about going back." — a copywriter who works remotely and runs the river trail on weekday mornings

oneBed.headline: 1BR Fort Langley — walk to the village
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom a short walk from Fort Langley village. Updated kitchen, quiet building, good storage. Utilities included. Available immediately.

twoBed.bodyText: Two-bedroom in a character home with private garden. Hardwood floors, updated throughout. All utilities included. Available now.

threeBed.headline: 3BR Fort Langley — near the fort, quiet street
threeBed.bodyText: Three-bedroom on a quiet Fort Langley street near the national historic site. Two bathrooms, good yard. Utilities included. Available immediately.

name: White Rock
tagline: Beachfront promenade, seaside town scale, a long way south
personalityDescription:
White Rock has a promenade, a pier, and a beach that gets genuinely warm in August. The town above the hill has its own rhythm — slower, more settled, more seasonal than anything in Metro Vancouver. In July it's full of day-trippers from the city. In February it's very quiet, and the people who are there chose to be.

People who are drawn to the idea of living near the ocean more than the idea of living in a city. Retirees, people working remotely, anyone making a deliberate move away from urban density toward something quieter and more specific. White Rock is for people who have answered the city question and moved on to a different question.

An hour minimum to downtown Vancouver — plan on it and decide if it works before you sign a lease. The town is small and the social options reflect that. Winters are quieter than you expect; the summer crowds are larger than you'd like.

analogousComparisons.default: A seaside town that happens to be inside a metropolitan area — for people who need both sentences to be true.

communityQuote: "I walk the pier every morning at 7am. In eight years I've missed maybe twenty days. That's why I'm here." — a retired teacher who moved from Burnaby

oneBed.headline: 1BR White Rock — steps from the promenade
oneBed.bodyText: One-bedroom a short walk from the White Rock promenade. Updated kitchen, ocean glimpses from the living room. Utilities included. Available immediately.
